title: Dog Hock Calluses: Causes, Care, and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Learn why dogs develop hard calluses on their hocks, how to manage them at home, and why these pressure sores are usually not an emergency.
content_html:
Understanding Hock Calluses in Dogs

A hard, thickened patch of skin on your dog's hock is typically a pressure sore, also known as a pressure callus. These develop as a protective response; when a dog frequently rests on hard surfaces like hardwood or tile, the skin thickens to shield the underlying bone from impact and friction. This is especially common in large or heavy-bodied breeds.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for a standard hock callus is Low. In most cases, these are purely cosmetic and do not cause your dog any pain or discomfort. However, you should contact a veterinarian if you notice signs of secondary infection, such as bleeding, pus, extreme heat, or if the dog begins to limp or lick the area excessively.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photo of the affected area is a vital step in triaging your dog's condition. A photograph allows a veterinary professional to differentiate between a simple callus and more concerning issues like a tumor, a cyst, or a hygroma (a fluid-filled sac). Providing a visual reference helps determine if the skin is healthy and intact or if medical intervention is required.

faqs.question: How can I prevent my dog's calluses from getting worse?
faqs.answer: The best way to manage calluses is to provide soft, padded surfaces for your dog to sleep on. Use orthopedic beds or rugs to minimize the pressure on their joints when they lie down.

faqs.question: Can I use human moisturizer on my dog's callus?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended to use human lotions, as many contain fragrances or chemicals that are toxic if licked. Use only pet-safe paw balms or veterinary-approved moisturizers.

faqs.question: Will the hair ever grow back on the callus?
faqs.answer: Once a thick callus has formed, the hair follicles in that area are usually damaged. While the skin may soften with better bedding, the hair often does not grow back.
